evidence: We use AI technologies in some of our business processes, including some consumer-facing features.
Developing our own AI technologies requires resources to develop, test, and maintain such technologies, which could be costly.
We use AI technologies in some of our business processes, including some consumer-facing features.
Adding AI technologies, especially generative AI, in both new and existing business processes may introduce additional risks.
Threat actors are increasingly leveraging AI to enhance the sophistication, speed, and effectiveness of cyberattacks... our security measures may not keep pace with these evolving capabilities.
Using AI technologies may lead to accuracy issues, security vulnerabilities, or biases... adversely impact our reputation, business, financial condition and results of operations.
We may also fail to adopt AI technologies at an appropriate pace, which could put us at a competitive disadvantage.

limitations: ai_investment_intensity: No dollar amounts, headcount, or quantified resource commitments tied to AI are disclosed; named use of AI in business processes and own development mentioned but unquantified, limiting score to 3.

ai_narrative_concreteness: AI use cases described only at a high level ('some business processes', 'consumer-facing features') with no deployment metrics or operational detail; generative AI mentioned aspirationally in risk context.

ai_risk_disclosure_depth: Risk-section material is present and discusses AI risks with named mechanisms (accuracy issues, security vulnerabilities, regulatory scrutiny, competitive disadvantage, cyberattack enablement), supporting score 4; no quantified risk exposure or named governance/oversight bodies found in excerpts, which would be required for score 5.
